text: The surgeon performs an initial diagnostic laparoscopy, visually inspecting the liver, small bowel, and colon.
The surgeon releases the sigmoid colon from its congenital adhesions and peritoneal attachments to the lateral pelvic wall, freeing it from surrounding structures.
This can be done at the beginning of the surgery as we are doing in this case or at the completion of the mobilization of the left colon and splenic flexure.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the peritoneal attachments of the sigmoid colon, ensuring it is free from tension for the upcoming extracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon displays the freed sigmoid colon, revealing the underlying gonadal vessels, iliac vessels, iliopsoas muscle, and left ureter, all covered by retroperitoneal fascia.
The surgeon extends the incision on the lateral peritoneum cranially from the sigmoid colon onto the descending colon, reaching up to the splenic flexure.
The surgeon incises the peritoneal fold without performing a lateral to medial dissection.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure and descending colon from a medial to lateral approach, similar to the technique used for right colon mobilization.
The surgeon displaces the omentum upwards into the supracolic compartment, clearing the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon lifts the splenic flexure of the colon, creating a tenting effect that prominently displays the left colic vessels.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum above and below the left collic vessels, entering the loose areolar tissue plane between the retroperitoneal fascia and the colonic mesentery.
The white line of Toldt is clearly visible, guiding the plane of dissection along the lateral aspect of the colon.
The surgeon develops the avascular space using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, separating tissue layers within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection at the level of the splenic flexure, revealing the bluish tinge of the lower polar spleen covered by the peritoneum.

The surgeon continues the median to lateral dissection at the level of the descending colon, pushing the retroperitoneal fascia downward to join the dissection plane with the already incised lateral peritoneum.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the descending colon and begins the cranial dissection, shifting focus to the upper regions of the surgical field.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum of the transverse mesocolon and continues the dissection in a cranial direction, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon encounters the body and tail of the pancreas during the dissection, visible beneath the peritoneal layer.
During the superior dissection, the surgeon carefully avoids entering the retro pancreatic plane, maintaining focus on the intended anatomical layers.

The surgeon carefully dissects anterior to the pancreas, avoiding entry into the retro pancreatic plane, similar to the technique used in right colon mobilization where the dissection stays anterior to the duodenum.
If we enter the retropancreatic plane, which may easily be a common mistake for the beginners, we would not just be in the wrong plane but risk injury to the splenic and the renal vein.
The surgeon divides the transverse mesocolon attachment at the inferior border of the pancreas, entering the lesser sac.

The surgeon proceeds from medial to lateral, freeing the peritoneal attachment on the anterior surface of the inferior border of the pancreas and dividing the retroperitoneal attachment of the splenic flexure.
At the splenic flexure,
The surgeon applies traction, causing the tail of the pancreas to lift towards the splenic hilum, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
Here it is very important to avoid any injury to the tail of the pancreas.
The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection, having divided the peritoneal and retroperitoneal attachments at the splenic flexure and entered the lesser sac.
The surgeon clears the left colic vessels of peritoneum and fibro fatty tissue, then applies double clips and divides the vessels at their origin.
The surgeon clips and divides a proximally arising first sigmoid branch from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), ensuring careful handling of the vessel.
The surgeon mobilizes the descending colon specimen, preparing it for retrieval through the extracorporeal incision and subsequent anastomosis.
The surgeon divides the linocolic ligament, separating it from surrounding tissues to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon joins the lateral and medial planes of dissection, completing the separation of the splenic flexure and descending colon from the retroperitoneal geratous fascia, pancreas, and lower pole of the spleen.
The surgeon dissects the transverse colon from the mid transverse colon to the splenic flexure, freeing it from omental attachments to facilitate its delivery into the midline wound.
The left colon is fully mobilized, showing clear separation from surrounding structures, ready for retrieval through the extracorporeal incision.
The surgeon exposes the tumor located at the splenic flexure, which is visibly characterized by a large umbilication on its surface.
The surgeon delivers the resected colon specimen through a midline incision using a wound protector. An extracorporeal stapled anastomosis is performed to reconnect the bowel.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the sigmoid colon, ensuring adequate separation to facilitate the delivery of the distal colon segment into the abdominal wound.
